summary: Gruber examines the rumors of Apple switching to Intel processors days before WWDC 2005, raising skeptical questions about the reports from CNet and The Wall Street Journal. He notes a semiconductor analyst's claim that Intel chips would actually cost Apple more than IBM/Freescale processors. He questions the lack of technical details in the reports, suggests the leak may have been strategically planned to build WWDC hype, and mocks Robert Scoble's vague 'confirmation.' Gruber's own prediction is that Intel will produce PowerPC chips for Apple rather than Apple switching to x86, though he hedges with a one-dollar bet.
key_points: A semiconductor analyst argues Apple pays less for IBM/Freescale chips than Intel would charge, undermining the cost-saving rationale for switching
Neither CNet nor the WSJ provided any technical details about the supposed switch, which Gruber finds suspicious
The Friday-before-WWDC timing suggests a deliberate leak designed to generate keynote buzz
Switching to little-endian x86 would create significant byte-order headaches for developers whose code assumes big-endian PowerPC
Gruber's prediction: Intel will produce PowerPC chips for Apple, not a full architecture switch
money_quotes: On Friday morning, no one was talking about WWDC; by Friday night, everyone was.
How could someone 'confirm' this story but yet not know the answers to these questions? I suspect that if Apple announces anything related to Intel, Scoble will claim he was in on it.
Whatever they do announce will have to compete for headline space with 'Apple Not Switching to Intel Chips'.
Here's my bet: Intel is going to produce PowerPC chips for Apple. But I'm only betting one dollar.
Switching to the little-endian x86 would be extremely irritating for any developer with a code base that makes assumptions about byte order.
key_insight: The Apple-Intel rumors lack technical substance, and the strategic timing of the leak suggests Apple is orchestrating buzz rather than genuinely having its plans exposed.
